---
title: "Analysis of Paired Data: Beyond The T-Test"
date: 2026-03-27
code-fold: false
echo: false
fig-cap-location: top
categories: []
number-sections: false
draft: true
cache: false
format:
  html:
    html-math-method: mathjax
---



## Warm Up: Pairs of Continuous Data

Let the $y_{c, k}$ be some continuous outcome from the $k^{th}$ observation in pair $c$.  The $c^{th}$ pair is then $(y_{c, 1}, y_{c, 2})$.  If we want to test the null hypothesis that $E[y_{c, 1}] = E[y_{c, 2}]$ then the typical advice is to use a paired t-test.

The paired nature of the data leverages any correlation between the two observations to (hopefully) reduce the width of the confidence interval. This can readily be seen by computing the variance of the within-pair difference
$$
\beta_c = y_{c, 2} - y_{c, 1}.
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\beta_c) = \operatorname{Var}(y_{c, 2}) + \operatorname{Var}(y_{c, 1}) - 2\operatorname{Cov}(y_{c, 2}, y_{c, 1})
$$

If the two are not negatively correlated then $\operatorname{Var}(\beta_c) \leq \operatorname{Var}(y_{c, 2}) + \operatorname{Var}(y_{c, 1})$, and therefore the confidence intervals are smaller than had the data been treated as not paired.

Rather than treating the two samples separately, we can view the problem as estimating the mean of these paired differences. Let
$$
\beta = E[\beta_c]
$$
denote the population average difference.

A natural estimator is then
$$
\hat\beta = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{c=1}^n \beta_c = \bar{y}_2 - \bar{y}_1 \>.
$$

The variance can be estimated as

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\hat\beta)
=
\frac{1}{n(n-1)} \sum_{c=1}^n \left[(y_{c,2} - y_{c,1}) - (\bar{y}_2 - \bar{y}_1)\right]^2
$$

which has a cleaner interpretation as the variance of the $\beta_c$ about their mean

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\hat\beta)
=
\frac{1}{n(n-1)} \sum_{c=1}^n (\beta_c - \hat\beta)^2 \>.
$$


This seems like a lot of ink to spill on what feels like a simple topic, but I promise this is important and we will come back to it in the next section.


## Paired T-test and Regression Equivalence

It is often noted that the same inference obtained from a (non-)paired t-test can also be recovered from a regression model. The natural question is: can we obtain the result of a paired t-test from a regression *without* first taking differences? The answer is yes, provided we treat each pair as a cluster and use a cluster-robust standard error.

Let $c \in \mathcal{C}$ index clusters (here, pairs), and let $X$ be the design matrix with a column of ones for the intercept and an indicator $\mathbb{I}(k=2)$. If we adopt the convention that the observation with $k=2$ is always the second row, then

$$
X_c =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 \\
1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\quad \forall c \in \mathcal{C},
$$

and

$$
y_c =
\begin{bmatrix}
y_{c,1} \\
y_{c,2}
\end{bmatrix}
\quad \forall c \in \mathcal{C}.
$$

The cluster-robust variance estimator is then

$$
\hat V(\hat \beta)
=
(X^\prime X)^{-1}
\left(
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} X_c^\prime \hat \Omega_c X_c
\right)
(X^\prime X)^{-1}.
$$

Because our setup is quite straightforward, we can simplify some of these expressions.  First, let's tackle the "bread" of this sandwich estimator.  It can be shown that

$$ X^\prime X = n \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$ (X^\prime X)^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{n} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \>.$$

Next, if we denote $y_c - X_c \hat \beta = \begin{bmatrix} y_{c, 1} - \bar y_1, y_{c, 2} - \bar y_2   \end{bmatrix}^\prime =  \begin{bmatrix} \hat e_{c, 1}, \hat e_{c, 2} \end{bmatrix} ^\prime = \hat e_c$ then we can show that

$$ X_c^\prime \hat e_c = \begin{bmatrix} \hat e_{c, 1} + \hat e_{c,2} \\ \hat e_{c, 2} \end{bmatrix} \>.  $$

Rather than multiply the bread by the meat, let's first notice that

$$ (X^\prime X)^{-1} X^\prime_c \hat e_c = \dfrac{1}{n} \begin{bmatrix} \hat e_{c, 1} \\  \hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1} \end{bmatrix} \ $$

which _greatly_ simplifies the covariance to

$$\hat V(\hat \beta) = \dfrac{1}{n^2} \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}}
\begin{bmatrix} \hat e_{c, 1} \\  \hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \hat e_{c, 1} &  \hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1} \end{bmatrix}
 = \dfrac{1}{n^2} \begin{bmatrix} 
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \hat e_{c, 1}^2 & \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \hat e_{c, 1}(\hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1}) \\ 
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \hat e_{c, 1}(\hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1}) & \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} (\hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1})^2 
\end{bmatrix}$$

The bottom right element is what we want to look at.  Note

$$\begin{align}
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} (\hat e_{c, 2} - \hat e_{c, 1})^2 &= \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}}\left((y_{c, 2} - \bar{y}_2) - (y_{c, 1} - \bar{y}_1)\right)^2 \\
& = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \left( (y_{c, 2} - y_{c, 1}) - (\bar y_2 - \bar y_1) \right)^2 \\
& = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} (\beta_c - \hat \beta)^2
\end{align}$$

Which is approximately equal to the estimator for the variance we derived above (as $n$ gets large, the difference becomes negligible).


## Binary Data (or, When You Can't Take a Difference)
